items.rationale: His flagship technical conviction: building the fastest, simplest open-source zkVM using sumcheck/lookups, which he believes is becoming the dominant design that other teams quietly copy.
items.age_note: Consistent and intensifying from 2024-03 (Jolt launch) through 2025-09; most recent is strongest.

citations.quote: Jolt just got 6x faster. And simpler. It's probably the fastest zkVM cycle-for-cycle by a wide margin.
Several smart teams have already quietly adapted their zkVMs to look more like Jolt. I believe Jolt will be obvious next year.

items.rationale: He sees lack of privacy as the single biggest friction for institutional adoption, and wants cryptographic privacy (balances, transfers, addresses) as the default across the financial stack.

citations.quote: the lack of privacy onchain is one of the biggest sources of friction, if not a dealbreaker, for many institutions. It's critical.
We have the technology to make radical privacy the default. Let's build it.

items.rationale: Believes AI bots break airdrops and social networks; a private, unique 'root of trust' ID (he favors World ID) is needed so humans and good bots can coexist and rewards reach real people.

citations.quote: Every social network will struggle. Every person will want a network where you can tell the difference.
I believe that World ID is the most promising as the root of trust.

items.rationale: Core to his worldview: tokens that confer ownership of decentralized network-marketplaces ('the king'), funded by protocol revenue with built-in buyback/burn mechanisms — now finally economically and legally viable.

citations.quote: Network tokens (the king 👑) vs. company-backed tokens (not so much).
the economic model of ~every network token should include a complete buyback mechanism

items.rationale: Long-held goal he thinks is finally arriving; excited by stablecoin growth decoupled from rates and by protocols like x402 enabling micropayments — though he is sharply critical of current payment UX (see concerns).
items.age_note: Theme since 2024-04; most recent enthusiasm is for x402/micropayments (2025-09).

citations.quote: x402 means micropayments might finally be a real business model online
stablecoin growth (including total supply, not pictured here) also seems relatively independent of interest rates. Very interesting!

items.rationale: Sees agents transacting/paying for their own compute onchain as inevitable, and neutral portable agent identity as an obvious unsolved challenge worth building.

citations.quote: Identity for agents is one of the most "obvious" future challenges for AI, yet there is still no clear effort to solve the problem.
agents being able to pay each other, and even for their own compute, is what I had in mind here. It will definitely happen.

items.rationale: Wants to wire impact/credit signals into payment for OSS authors (backed Merit Systems); calls Terminal the biggest OSS-funding improvement of his career.

citations.quote: Terminal is probably the biggest single improvement to open source software funding I've seen in my career.
Merit Systems was founded to design this: a protocol for measuring impact, attributing credit, and routing payment to the authors creating value

items.rationale: Repeatedly champions symbolic-testing tool Halmos for EVM security; used it to formally verify Pectra contracts and pushes adoption.

citations.quote: If you want to make sure your EVM code is secure, just get on board and use Halmos.
Karma is being modest — huge update for Halmos!

items.rationale: Argues L1 demand for blockspace is effectively unlimited and Ethereum cannot afford to slow down; actively supports Helios light-client work for trust-minimized verification.

citations.quote: More blockspace is good. L1 fees are not going to zero. We don't (and will never have) "enough".
Big update for your favorite Ethereum light client, Helios!

items.rationale: Believes crypto should abandon the two-company 'foundation' model now that DUNAs let decentralized projects cut complexity without compromising decentralization.

citations.quote: It's time for crypto move on from the "foundation" model.
When a DAO needs a legal entity, the native form is a DUNA. DAOs don't always need entities.

items.rationale: Views memecoins as technically uninteresting value-redistribution that deters great founders, hands ammo to regulators, and damages crypto's public perception — even as he now frames them analytically in his token taxonomy.
items.age_note: Peak alarm in 2024-04; by 2025 the tone shifts from polemic to taxonomic critique, but skepticism persists and is restated.

citations.quote: we shouldn't pretend that the casino doesn't set us back. Deterring great founders and giving ammo to politicized regulators has a profound effect.
Why is creating memecoins the best monetization strategy for creators?

items.rationale: Warns that tokens whose value comes uniquely from a single controlling company are not decentralized network tokens, carry security-like risk, and are often mislabeled to holders' detriment.

citations.quote: If there is only one company with actual control of the system... this is not a (decentralized) network token. It is something else and you may not like the answer.
What makes a token company-backed isn't that a company issued it, but that the value comes uniquely from the company

items.rationale: Believes AI now makes bot farms indistinguishable from humans, professionalizing reward farming and eroding social-network signal — a threat requiring new ID infrastructure to counter.

citations.quote: advances in AI mean identifying humans through behavior alone is cooked
I'm no longer sure whether replies are written by people most of the time.

items.rationale: Identified through hands-on testing that bridges take unpredictable cuts and leave dust, breaking exact-amount payments and composable payment workflows; sees this as a blocker for real crypto payments.
items.age_note: Concentrated, vivid concern from his Aug 2025 testing thread.

citations.quote: I don't see how crypto payments actually can work without deterministic outputs
Bridging takes an unpredictable cut, so you have to send more than 1000. And you'll end up with dust left over

items.rationale: Frustrated that merchants/users face network-name confusion and unnecessary KYC honeypots (e.g., BitPay demanding a driver's license), when cryptographic tools could minimize data collection.

citations.quote: forced to make a Bitpay account, and then asked to upload driver's license. Absolute joke and completely unnecessary.
Why are we forcing institutions to collect honeypots of sensitive, slowly-changing personal data?

items.rationale: Laments PC software shifting from open downloads to gatekept app stores, eroding the openness that makes general-purpose computers (and by analogy open networks) valuable.

citations.quote: PC apps moving from direct downloads to app stores is a regression, cursed, tragic.

items.rationale: His model example of a healthy network token: value accrues via EIP-1559 burn from blockspace sales (automatic buyback), supply isn't the commodity, and unlimited blockspace demand supports it.

citations.quote: The value accrues to ETH through the burn.
I think ETH is already doing (fully automatic) buybacks via the EIP-1559 burn.

items.rationale: Cites as a 'clear network token' that can capture protocol fees via a mechanism like UniStaker once the fee switch is turned on — ownership of a decentralized network-marketplace.

citations.quote: UNI is a clear network token
UNI can capture fees from the network (the switch is off right now) via a mechanism like UniStaker.

items.rationale: Defends Bitcoin as not a memecoin because it is the native asset of the most secure, decentralized network; admires it while noting 'Bitcoin envy' produces bad takes.

citations.quote: being the native asset of what is arguably the most secure and decentralized network gives it economic characteristics... unlike any memecoin. So I don't think it's a memecoin.
It's incredible that we have bitcoin.

items.rationale: Views the token as bootstrapping the privacy-preserving ID network he champions, with potential to capture fees automatically as commercial entities pay to use the identity system.

citations.quote: The tokens are to help bootstrap the ID network, and in the future may capture fees automatically.

items.rationale: Groups Solana with ETH and UNI as a legitimate network token able to capture value from its underlying network via protocol fees; positive but less individually emphasized.

citations.quote: Network tokens like ETH, SOL, and UNI can capture value from their underlying networks through protocol fees.

items.rationale: His canonical example of a company-backed token whose value came uniquely from a single company — the category he treats as security-like and fundamentally weaker than network tokens.

citations.quote: FTT was one, there are others.

items.rationale: Invokes LUNA as the cautionary failure for algorithmic stablecoins; welcomes more experiments but explicitly wants to avoid repeating it.

citations.quote: With increasingly obvious product-market fit for stablecoins, I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).

items.rationale: Classifies Dogecoin as a memecoin/store-of-value token with no enterprise value or protocol revenue — structurally inferior to value-capturing network tokens.
items.age_note: Stated 2024-12-05 in a taxonomy aside; consistent with his ongoing memecoin stance but not individually restated since.

citations.quote: memecoins/store of value tokens like Dogecoin have no enterprise value (like gold).

risk_regime.rationale: Eddy is emphatically builder-bullish: he calls this the best-ever time to start a crypto project, cites a transformed regulatory environment and a '$500b GO button', and says we can finally build economically sustainable decentralized networks. His optimism is conditional and quality-gated — he wants fundamentals, network tokens, and privacy, and is scathing about memecoin/casino activity and broken payment UX — but his overall posture toward building and deploying into crypto is strongly forward-leaning. He explicitly distrusts privately-cynical 'bullposters'.

notes: View is that of a deeply technical investor/builder (a16z crypto CTO), so 'risk posture' reflects conviction about what to build, not short-term token-price calls. Strongest, most recent signals (2025): zkVMs/Jolt, onchain privacy, Sybil-resistant private identity, and network-token economics with buyback/fee-switch mechanisms. A clear reversal over the corpus: regulatory environment went from a major lament to a celebrated tailwind ('the regulatory environment has changed completely', 2025-01-08), which underpins his risk-on stance. Memecoin concern softened in form (polemic → taxonomy) but not in substance. The Aug-2025 cross-chain payments thread is his most concrete tactical frustration. Tokens are named in his own framework of 'network tokens' (good) vs 'company-backed' and 'arcade'/memecoins (suspect); he treats ETH/UNI/SOL as network tokens and BTC as a sui-generis secure store of value. He repeatedly stresses he will never monetize a16z's open-source work, so 'excitement' about Jolt/Helios/Halmos is public-good advocacy, not a token bet.
